owner_information.owners: Michael R. Donovan and Sarah L. Donovan, Trustees of the Donovan Family Trust

property_information.legal_description_of_property: Lot 7, Block 3, Willow Creek Meadows Subdivision, being part of the NE 1/4 of the SW 1/4 of Section 14, Township 8 North, Range 10 East, City of Maple Grove, Dane County, Wisconsin, according to the recorded plat thereof.
property_information.current_zoning: R-4 Medium Density Residential (up to 12 units/acre)

request_details.describe_reason_for_request:
I am requesting that the zoning designation for the subject property at 1502 Willow Creek Road be changed from R-4 Medium Density Residential (up to 12 units per acre) to R-1 Low Density Residential (single-family, up to 2 units per acre).

When my wife Sarah and I purchased this 4.85-acre parcel in 2016, the surrounding area was largely semi-rural with a mix of small farms, single-family homes on larger lots, and a few older duplexes. Over the past several years, there has been increasing interest from developers to assemble parcels in this corridor for townhome and small apartment projects under the existing R-4 zoning. While I understand and respect the community’s need for a variety of housing types, the R-4 classification on this particular parcel no longer reflects how the land is actually being used, nor does it fit our long-term intentions as owners.

We built our single-family home on the southern portion of the property in 2018 and have been using the remainder of the land as a large yard, orchard, and garden space. We have invested significantly in landscaping, planting over 30 fruit trees (apple, pear, and cherry), installing a small pollinator meadow along the western boundary, and constructing a detached garage and workshop that are consistent with low-density residential use. Our two children, ages 9 and 12, attend the nearby Maple Grove Elementary, and we walk or bike with them along Willow Creek Road; increased traffic from a higher-density development would directly impact their safety and the safety of other neighborhood children who use the same route.

In talking with immediate neighbors on Willow Creek Drive and Willow Creek Road, there is strong concern that if we were to sell the property under the current R-4 zoning, a developer could pursue a 20–30 unit townhome project. This would be out of character with the surrounding developed lots, which are predominantly single-family homes on half-acre to one-acre parcels. Several neighbors have expressed to us, in person and via email, that they chose this neighborhood specifically because of its quieter, more spacious feel compared to other parts of Maple Grove that already have townhomes and small apartment buildings. A downzoning to R-1 would align the zoning with the actual character and expectations of the existing neighborhood.

From an infrastructure standpoint, the current utilities and road network are more appropriate for low-density residential than for the full build-out potential of R-4. Willow Creek Road is a relatively narrow two-lane street with open ditches and no dedicated turn lanes. We already see occasional congestion during school drop-off and pick-up times. A higher-density project would likely require road widening, additional stormwater management infrastructure, and potentially signalization at the Willow Creek Road and County Highway M intersection. These improvements would be costly, and it is not clear that they are in the city’s capital plans for this segment. By contrast, continuing the existing pattern of single-family homes on larger lots will result in a more gradual and manageable increase in traffic and demand on utilities.

Environmentally, the property contains a shallow swale along the northern edge that conveys stormwater toward the Willow Creek wetland complex. While this swale is not mapped as a regulated wetland, we have observed seasonal standing water and a variety of frogs, songbirds, and pollinators utilizing this area. We have intentionally left this portion of the property more natural, with native grasses and wildflowers, to provide habitat and help with infiltration. A higher-density development would almost certainly require more extensive grading and a larger percentage of impervious surface, increasing runoff volumes and potentially impacting the downstream wetland. A downzoning to R-1 would help preserve more pervious area and allow us to continue managing the land in an environmentally sensitive way.

Economically, we recognize that downzoning may reduce the speculative market value of the property for intensive development. However, our intent is not to maximize short-term profit but to maintain our home and land as a long-term residence and, eventually, to be able to divide off at most one additional single-family lot for a family member or for sale to an owner-occupant. We are comfortable with the trade-off of reduced development potential in exchange for greater certainty that the property will remain low-density and compatible with our home and the surrounding neighborhood. We have spoken with a local real estate agent, who confirmed that there is steady demand for single-family lots in this part of Maple Grove and that R-1 zoning would not make the property unusable or unmarketable in the future.

We have also consulted the city’s Comprehensive Plan, adopted in 2020. The Future Land Use Map designates this area as "Suburban Residential," which, according to the plan, is intended primarily for single-family homes with limited duplexes. The descriptive text emphasizes transitions and compatibility between new development and existing neighborhoods. Given that the built-out character of Willow Creek Meadows Subdivision is almost entirely single-family homes, we believe that R-1 zoning more accurately implements the intent of the Comprehensive Plan for this specific block and will reduce pressure for abrupt changes in scale and intensity.

On a personal level, our family is deeply rooted in this community. I run a small residential remodeling business based out of our home office, and my wife Sarah works part-time as a nurse at Maple Grove Clinic. We host neighborhood gatherings in our backyard several times a year, and we have invested time and effort into making our property a welcoming, stable presence on the street. We are not seeking this downzoning to block any particular project—there is no active development application on our parcel at this time—but rather as a proactive step to ensure that the zoning better matches our long-term use and the neighborhood’s expectations. We want to avoid future conflicts or misunderstandings if and when we eventually transfer ownership of the property to our children or another single-family homeowner.

In summary, the requested downzoning from R-4 to R-1 will:
1. Bring the zoning into alignment with the existing and intended single-family use of the property.
2. Enhance compatibility with the surrounding neighborhood’s established low-density character.
3. Reduce potential traffic and infrastructure strain on Willow Creek Road and nearby intersections.
4. Support ongoing environmental stewardship of on-site drainage features and habitat areas.
5. Provide long-term predictability for us, our neighbors, and the city regarding the scale and type of any future development.

For these reasons, we respectfully request approval of this downzoning application and are happy to provide any additional documentation, including photographs of the property, letters from neighbors, or a more detailed narrative, if that would assist staff and the Plan Commission in their review.
